# Transcrição: 08- 41 Where Oh Where

- **Fonte:** 08- 41 Where Oh Where
- **Duração:** 07:30
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

A few years ago, I was getting ready to compete in a Moth Story Slam. These competitions have a theme, and the theme of this competition was kitchen. And oddly enough, I could not find a story in my pile of stories that related to that theme. I had a couple kitchen stories that I'd already told, but I needed a new one. And for the first time in a very long time, I couldn't find a story. I could have canceled the competition, but I had a bunch of friends coming, so I felt sort of obligated and pressured to find a real good kitchen story. And so in a panic, I sat down one day and I listed all the kitchens of my life. My childhood kitchen, the first apartment, the first apartment Alicia and I lived in, the kitchens and restaurants where I worked, I made a list of all of the kitchens I had ever occupied. And then I placed myself mentally back into each one of those kitchens to try to look around and see if I could find a story. I found 27 stories that took place in kitchens. I recovered 27 memories from kitchens in the past by placing myself inside those kitchens by making a list of them and then returning to them. What I discovered through that exercise is that stories hide in places. That whatever happens to you in life, any story you tell, it always happens in a physical location. You are always somewhere when something happens. And so if we acknowledge that, in order to find stories from our past, we have to simply go back to a place, imagine that place in our mind's eye, put ourselves there and then look around and see what we can see. I remember when I visited Laura's kitchen, my high school girlfriend, I stood in that kitchen and suddenly I remembered that Laura's favorite after school snack was a scoop of peanut butter with an injection of maple syrup. She would have an enormous scoop of peanut butter on a spoon and she would shoot maple syrup into the middle of it and eat it. Now that's not a story for the stage, but that's something I can tell her daughter someday. And it was something I was so happy to return to me. I found a wonderful memory of telling my uncle to be nicer to my mother on Thanksgiving one year, a memory I had completely forgotten. But one of those first moments in my life when as a younger boy, I told a man something and he actually listened to me. I shamed him and I remember feeling so victorious in shaming him, a memory completely forgotten

## [03:03 - 06:05]

until I placed myself in my aunt's kitchen and looked around for a story. The story I settled on for that moth story slam was a story that took place in a kitchen in an apartment I owned when Alicia and I began dating. I was supposed to make her an avocado melt and I failed because I had never touched an avocado before. And so when I finally figured out how to open it after calling a friend for help, I discovered it wasn't ripe. And so when she came in, I had nothing to feed her, but she discovered a recipe on the counter, the recipe from a restaurant called Martins in the Berkshires. She told me that Martins had the best avocado melts in the world. So I had gotten in touch with Martins back in the time when that was not easy. There was no, you know, take a picture on a cell phone and text it to me. I had to call them and ask them to photocopy the recipe like under a photocopy or and put it in an envelope and stamp it and mail it to me. I had the recipe on the counter next to the failed meal. And Alicia, being who she is, she thought that the recipe was more than enough that I had listened to her, that I had put in an effort that I had the recipe on the counter next to an avocado that I didn't know how to open and a lemon that I had yet to learn how to zest. But that didn't matter to her. It was the effort that mattered. That was the story I told that night. And I won that story slam. But that memory of the avocado melt and the recipe, all of that had been lost to me. A beautiful moment between my future wife and me lost until I placed myself in that cruddy little apartment and back in that cruddy little kitchen and looked around and said to myself, what's ever happened in this kitchen that might be story worthy? And I found that moment. Stories are just waiting for us in these places, in these locations. And when we put ourselves back in those locations mentally, we'll start to see the stories that we've left behind. Here is a list of locations that you can place yourself in. It's a list to get you started. It'll take you a while to get through this list, but eventually you will. But that should not be the end of you're aware. I play where aware all the time. Every time I find myself in a new location, one that I have yet to explore, I take a moment and make a list of all of them. All the locations like this one that I've been in before and I start looking for stories. My most recent addition was the post office. I was standing in line at the post office and it occurred to me, oh, I've never played where aware in post offices before. So I made a list of all the post offices I could ever recall occupying at any time.

## [06:05 - 07:30]

And then I placed myself mentally into each one of those post offices, hoping to find a story. Honestly, I did not. Nothing significant that I can recall has ever taken place in a post office in my life. But I tried. I tried to find a story. And the next time I'm in a place like that again, I'll try again. Most of the time I find a new story. I find a new story because stories are hiding in places because every story happens in a place. All we have to do is make a list of those places and then get our mind's eye back into that place. Look around and ask yourself, what has ever happened here? Is anything here story worthy? Is anything here worth remembering? Is anything here worth grabbing and bringing to the present so that I can feel happier, more complete so my life can feel fuller? Play where aware. Play out in the world. But start with my list. It's a good list. It's an enormous variety of locations. Spend time in each one and find some new stories. Good luck.
